conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by gathering some basic information:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex who is 14, and Blake who is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me start with some questions about your situation:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do they live with you and you're responsible for their care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't live with me at the moment - they live elsewhere. But Blake does live at home with me, yes.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to understand each child's situation separately.

Let me ask about Blake first, since they live with you:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training? For example, are they enrolled in full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels or equivalent) or an approved training program?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education - they're doing A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex, who doesn't live with you:

**Where is Alex living? For example, are they living with another parent, a relative, in care, or in hospital?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand this situation better:

**Does the other parent contribute more towards Alex's upkeep than you do? For example, are they providing most of the day-to-day care and financial support for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they're the one providing the day-to-day care since Alex lives with them. They're looking after Alex full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now let me ask about you personally:

**Are you living in the UK, and are you either a UK citizen or have the right to live in the UK (such as having settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another immigration status that allows you to claim benefits)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I have one more important question:

**Do either you or your partner (if you have one) have an individual income over £50,000 per year?**

(This doesn't affect eligibility to *receive* Child Benefit, but it may affect whether there's a tax charge to pay back some or all of it.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have a partner, and my income is below £50,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me explain the outcome for each child:

**For Alex (aged 14):** Since Alex lives with their other parent who is providing the day-to-day care, the other parent is the one who is responsible for Alex. According to the rules, only one person can get Child Benefit for a child, and it would normally be the person the child lives with. You could potentially still claim if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep (including things like clothes, presents, food, pocket money), but since you've indicated the other parent is providing full-time care and support, they would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**For Blake (aged 19):** Blake lives with you and is in approved education (doing A-levels). You can get Child Benefit for a child under 20 if they stay in approved education or training, which Blake is doing. Since Blake lives with you and you're responsible for them, you would be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

You also won't face any High Income Child Benefit tax charge since your income is below £50,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I hope this assessment is helpful. If you have any questions about these outcomes or your specific circumstances, please feel free to ask!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16) but does not live with the claimant. Alex lives with their other parent who provides day-to-day care and support. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child, which usually means either living with them or paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep. Since the other parent provides full-time care and the claimant has indicated they do not contribute as much as the other parent, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and this would be the other parent who Alex lives with.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can claim for a child under 20 if they stay in approved education or training. Blake lives with the claimant who is responsible for their care. The claimant is a UK citizen living in the UK (Manchester) with income below £50,000. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake only, as Blake lives with them and is in approved education. The claimant is not eligible for Alex because Alex lives with their other parent who provides the day-to-day care, making that parent the responsible person for Child Benefit purposes.
